questions.stem: A 22-year-old male smoker, tall and thin, presents with sudden-onset right-sided pleuritic chest pain and mild dyspnoea. Oxygen saturations are 98% on air. Chest examination reveals reduced breath sounds and hyperresonance on the right. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: The combination of a young, tall, thin male smoker with acute pleuritic pain and ipsilateral reduced breath sounds with hyperresonance is classic for primary spontaneous pneumothorax. Asthma exacerbation would have wheeze, not unilateral hyperresonance; pulmonary embolism often has risk factors and raised JVP; pleurisy may have a friction rub; costochondritis is reproducible on palpation.

questions.stem: A 68-year-old man with known COPD (GOLD 3) presents with acute worsening of dyspnoea and right-sided chest pain. He is tachypnoeic, trachea is central, and there is hyperresonance and reduced breath sounds on the right. Chest X-ray shows a small pneumothorax. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: In a patient with COPD, acute deterioration with unilateral hyperresonance and reduced breath sounds is typical of secondary spontaneous pneumothorax. A COPD exacerbation does not cause unilateral hyperresonance; pneumonia presents with fever and crackles; pleural effusion is stony dull; pulmonary embolism may have pleuritic pain but not hyperresonance.

questions.stem: A 25-year-old male with no prior lung disease presents with severe dyspnoea and pleuritic chest pain after a sudden coughing fit. He is distressed, hypotensive, and has jugular venous distension. The trachea is deviated to the left. On the right, there is hyperresonance and absent breath sounds. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: Tracheal deviation away from the affected side with hypotension and JVD in an acute dyspnoeic patient is diagnostic of tension pneumothorax. Massive PE and cardiac tamponade cause JVD but no tracheal deviation; MI has cardiac pain; asthma has wheeze and no tracheal shift.

questions.stem: A 32-year-old woman presents with recurrent right-sided pleuritic chest pain and dyspnoea occurring within 48 hours of her menstrual period. She is a non-smoker. Chest X-ray shows a small right pneumothorax. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: The cyclical timing with menstruation is pathognomonic for catamenial pneumothorax due to thoracic endometriosis. Primary spontaneous pneumothorax is not cyclical; pulmonary embolism does not correlate with menses; viral pleurisy is not recurrent in this pattern; ruptured nodule is rare and not cyclical.

questions.stem: A 20-year-old woman with known asthma presents with acute severe dyspnoea and right-sided chest pain. On examination, she has widespread expiratory wheeze but also reduced breath sounds on the right. Peak expiratory flow is 30% of predicted. Chest X-ray shows a right pneumothorax. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
options: Secondary spontaneous pneumothorax due to asthma
Severe asthma exacerbation with mucus plugging
questions.explanation: The presence of a pneumothorax on chest X-ray in an asthmatic with acute dyspnoea indicates a secondary spontaneous pneumothorax. Tension pneumothorax would show tracheal deviation and hypotension; severe asthma exacerbation alone does not cause unilateral reduced breath sounds; PE and pneumonia do not typically cause hyperresonance or pneumothorax.

questions.stem: A 19-year-old thin male presents to ED with sudden-onset left-sided pleuritic chest pain and dyspnoea. On examination, breath sounds are reduced on the left. What is the most appropriate first investigation to confirm the diagnosis?
questions.explanation: Chest X-ray is the first-line investigation to confirm pneumothorax and estimate size. CT is reserved for complex cases or after drainage.

questions.stem: A 70-year-old man with COPD on home oxygen presents with progressive dyspnoea. He is unable to sit upright. A supine anteroposterior chest radiograph is equivocal for pneumothorax. What is the next most appropriate investigation?
questions.explanation: CT thorax is the gold standard to confirm equivocal pneumothorax, especially in supine patients where signs may be subtle. Repeat erect X-ray may be difficult or inconclusive.

questions.stem: A 25-year-old woman presents with her second spontaneous pneumothorax. She has no known lung disease. Which investigation is most appropriate to assess for an underlying cause?
questions.explanation: High-resolution CT thorax can detect subpleural blebs or bullae, which are common in primary spontaneous pneumothorax and may affect management.

questions.stem: A 17-year-old male presents with severe dyspnoea and tracheal deviation to the right. The left hemithorax is hyperresonant with absent breath sounds. A chest radiograph is obtained. Which radiological finding confirms the clinical suspicion of tension pneumothorax?
questions.explanation: Contralateral mediastinal shift is a definitive sign of tension pneumothorax on chest X-ray, indicating increased intrapleural pressure. Other signs are less specific.

questions.stem: A 65-year-old man on mechanical ventilation in the ICU develops sudden hypotension, oxygen desaturation, and increased airway pressures. A chest radiograph is not immediately available. What is the best bedside investigation to rapidly confirm or exclude a pneumothorax?
questions.explanation: Bedside lung ultrasound can rapidly detect absent lung sliding and lung point, with high sensitivity and faster than chest X-ray in this setting. Needle thoracostomy is therapeutic, not diagnostic.

questions.stem: A 22-year-old slim male presents with acute onset right-sided pleuritic chest pain. He is not short of breath. Examination reveals reduced breath sounds on the right. Chest X-ray shows a small apical pneumothorax with a 1.5 cm rim. What is the most appropriate first-line management?
questions.explanation: For a small (<2 cm rim), asymptomatic primary spontaneous pneumothorax, UK guidelines recommend conservative management (observation) as first-line. Aspiration or chest drain is reserved for larger or symptomatic pneumothoraces.

questions.stem: A 28-year-old man presents with sudden left-sided chest pain and dyspnoea. He has no significant past medical history. Chest X-ray shows a 3 cm rim pneumothorax. What is the most appropriate initial treatment?
options: Chest drain insertion with underwater seal
questions.explanation: For a moderate-to-large primary spontaneous pneumothorax with breathlessness, first-line treatment is aspiration. If unsuccessful, a chest drain is inserted.

questions.stem: A 35-year-old is brought to the emergency department after a road traffic collision. He is hypotensive, tachycardic, and has absent breath sounds on the right with tracheal deviation to the left. There is distended neck veins. What is the most appropriate first-line treatment?
options: Needle decompression at the 2nd intercostal space midclavicular line
questions.explanation: This is tension pneumothorax. Immediate needle decompression (2nd intercostal space, midclavicular line) is the first-line treatment to relieve tension, followed by chest drain insertion.

questions.stem: A 30-year-old woman presents with a second episode of left primary spontaneous pneumothorax within 6 months. She is haemodynamically stable and has a 2 cm rim on X-ray. What is the most appropriate definitive management to prevent recurrence?
questions.explanation: For recurrent primary spontaneous pneumothorax, definitive treatment to prevent recurrence is pleurodesis (commonly via VATS). Chest drain alone does not prevent recurrence.

questions.stem: A 65-year-old man with known COPD (FEV1 40% predicted) presents with acute dyspnoea and right-sided chest pain. Chest X-ray shows a 2.5 cm rim pneumothorax. He is mildly hypoxic. What is the most appropriate first-line management?
options: Chest drain insertion with underwater seal
questions.explanation: In secondary spontaneous pneumothorax (e.g., due to COPD), first-line treatment is chest drain insertion because aspiration has higher failure rates and recurrence is common.

questions.stem: A 22-year-old thin male presents to ED with acute onset of right-sided pleuritic chest pain and mild shortness of breath. Observations: RR 18, SpO2 98% on air, HR 80, BP 120/80. CXR shows a small apical pneumothorax (<2 cm rim). What is the most appropriate next step?
options: Insert a chest drain with underwater seal
Discharge with safety net advice and early outpatient follow-up
Request a CT thorax to assess for blebs
Admit for observation with high-flow oxygen
Perform needle aspiration of the pneumothorax
questions.explanation: NICE guidelines state that for a primary spontaneous pneumothorax with no significant breathlessness and a rim <2 cm, discharge with advice and follow-up is appropriate. Aspiration is indicated if >2 cm or breathless. Chest drain, CT, and admission are not first-line.

questions.stem: A 25-year-old man presents with a 2-day history of worsening dyspnoea and right pleuritic chest pain. He is breathless at rest. Observations: RR 24, SpO2 95% on air, HR 100, BP 110/70. CXR shows a moderate pneumothorax with a 3 cm rim. What is the most appropriate immediate next step?
options: Perform needle aspiration of the pneumothorax
Observe and repeat CXR in 4 hours
Administer high-flow oxygen via a non-rebreather mask
Request a CT thorax to evaluate lung parenchyma
Insert a chest drain with underwater seal
questions.explanation: For a symptomatic primary spontaneous pneumothorax >2 cm, needle aspiration is first-line according to NICE. Chest drain is reserved if aspiration fails. Oxygen, CT, or observation are not the immediate next actions in this symptomatic patient.

questions.stem: A 30-year-old male is brought to ED acutely dyspnoeic after onset of severe right chest pain. On examination, he is hypotensive (BP 85/50), trachea deviated to the left, distended neck veins, and absent breath sounds on the right. What is the most appropriate immediate next step?
options: Perform needle decompression of the right chest
Request an urgent CT thorax to confirm the diagnosis
Insert a large-bore chest drain in the 5th intercostal space mid-axillary line
Call the anaesthetic team for emergency intubation
questions.explanation: This is tension pneumothorax, a life-threatening emergency. Immediate needle decompression (14G cannula, 2nd intercostal space mid-clavicular line) is the priority before chest drain insertion. CT, intubation, or oxygen alone delay definitive management.

questions.stem: A 22-year-old woman is admitted for a right spontaneous pneumothorax initially managed with a chest drain. On day 3, the drain continues to bubble with expiration, and a repeat CXR shows the lung is not fully expanded. What is the most appropriate next step?
options: Refer to thoracic surgery for pleurodesis
Request a CT thorax to identify the air leak
Remove the chest drain and observe
Apply suction to the chest drain at high pressure
Check the chest drain position with a CXR
questions.explanation: A persistent air leak and non-expansion require first checking drain position (e.g., malposition, kinking) with a CXR. Suction may be considered if position is correct, but removal or CT/referral are premature without assessing the drain.

questions.stem: A 60-year-old man with known COPD and a previous spontaneous pneumothorax presents with mild dyspnoea. Observations: RR 20, SpO2 88% on air, HR 95, BP 130/80. CXR shows a small apical pneumothorax (<2 cm rim). What is the most appropriate next step?
options: Perform needle aspiration of the pneumothorax
Discharge with safety net advice and follow-up
Admit for observation and supplementary oxygen
Insert a chest drain with underwater seal
Request a CT thorax to assess for bullae
questions.explanation: In secondary spontaneous pneumothorax (e.g., COPD), even a small pneumothorax requires admission for observation and oxygen due to limited respiratory reserve. Needle aspiration or chest drain are not first-line for a small, minimally symptomatic secondary pneumothorax. Discharge is inappropriate. CT is not immediate.

questions.stem: A 22-year-old tall, thin male presents with acute-onset right-sided pleuritic chest pain and dyspnoea. On examination, breath sounds are reduced on the right. Which single clinical finding most specifically indicates a tension pneumothorax?
options: Reduced chest expansion on the right
Absent breath sounds on the right
Hyperresonance on percussion of the right chest
questions.explanation: Tracheal deviation away from the affected side is the hallmark of tension pneumothorax due to mediastinal shift. Absent breath sounds, hyperresonance, and reduced expansion are common in simple pneumothorax but are not specific for tension.

questions.stem: A 72-year-old man with severe COPD (FEV1 30% predicted) attended the emergency department 2 days ago with a small right-sided pneumothorax (rim <2 cm on CT) and was managed conservatively. He now presents with confusion. His heart rate is 105 bpm, blood pressure 95/60 mmHg, respiratory rate 30/min, oxygen saturation 88% on 15 L O2. Which vital sign abnormality most strongly suggests progression to tension pneumothorax?
questions.explanation: In a patient with known pneumothorax, hypotension indicates reduced cardiac output from mediastinal shift and impaired venous return, characteristic of tension. Tachycardia and tachypnoea are common but less specific; hypoxia is often non-specific in COPD.

questions.stem: A 28-year-old man presents with sudden left-sided pleuritic chest pain and dyspnoea. On examination, he has reduced breath sounds on the left, but also hypotension (85/50 mmHg) and dullness to percussion at the left base. A chest X-ray shows a left pneumothorax and a pleural effusion. What complication has likely occurred?
questions.explanation: The combination of pneumothorax, hypotension, and dullness to percussion suggests blood in the pleural space (haemopneumothorax), which can cause hypovolaemic shock. Tension pneumothorax typically produces hyperresonance, not dullness. Empyema would take longer to develop and usually presents with fever.

questions.stem: A 35-year-old woman presents with acute dyspnoea and central chest pain. An erect chest X-ray shows a left-sided pneumothorax. Which radiological finding is most indicative of a tension pneumothorax?
options: A small pneumothorax with a pleural adhesion
Deep sulcus sign on the left
Rim distance of 3 cm at the hilum
Subcutaneous emphysema in the left axilla
Mediastinal shift away from the left
questions.explanation: Mediastinal shift away from the pneumothorax is the cardinal radiological sign of tension, resulting from positive intrapleural pressure displacing the heart and trachea. The deep sulcus sign may suggest pneumothorax but not tension. Rim distance indicates size, not tension.

questions.stem: A 24-year-old man with a primary spontaneous pneumothorax has a chest X-ray showing a rim of 3 cm from the lung edge at the level of the hilum. He has mild dyspnoea. Which single feature on the X-ray indicates that this is a red flag requiring urgent chest drain insertion according to UK guidelines?
options: Subcutaneous emphysema along the chest wall
Presence of a small apical bleb
questions.explanation: NICE guidelines state that a primary spontaneous pneumothorax with a rim >2 cm on X-ray and significant symptoms should be drained. Rim distance >2 cm is the key threshold. The presence of blebs or subcutaneous emphysema does not itself mandate urgent drainage.
